**End-to-end Methodology Digitization Guide**


*Note this is an example guide for the purpose of teaching methodology digitalization utilising the Hedera Guardian. Standards act as the formal entity to approve or represent the correctness of a methodology as well as approve stakeholders and issuance, while digital tools can enable efficiencies and transparency in supporting the MRV process both in digital and digitalized formats.*

Digitizing a methodology can feel like a daunting task. Methodology documents often span anywhere from 60 to 400 pages, making them challenging to read and interpret. However, I’ve found that following a structured approach significantly simplifies the process of digitizing a methodology on the Guardian platform.

The first time I worked on digitizing a methodology, it took me a long time to complete, mainly because I wasn’t following best practices. Recently, I gained a clearer understanding of the digitization process, and since then, I’ve been able to make substantial progress within just a few days.

In this article, I’ll walk you through my journey of creating a [policy on Guardian](https://github.com/hashgraph/guardian/tree/main/Methodology%20Library/Gold%20Standard/Work%20In%20Progress/TPDDTEC%20v4.0) for the **TECHNOLOGIES AND PRACTICES TO DISPLACE DECENTRALIZED THERMAL ENERGY CONSUMPTION (TPDDTEC) v4.0** Gold Standard methodology. I’ll also outline the step-by-step process I followed from start to finish. I hope this serves as a helpful guide for anyone looking to digitize a methodology efficiently.

**Drafting the Policy Workflow**

The first step is to download the relevant methodology document from the carbon registry website. Gaining a solid understanding of the methodology—particularly its **applicability**, **scope**, **baseline and project scenarios**, and **emission reduction calculations**—is crucial for mapping out the workflow and schemas (including the calculation logic) for your digitized methodology.

Since **TPDDTEC v4.0** is a Gold Standard methodology, you can find its PDF on the [Gold Standard website](https://globalgoals.goldstandard.org/407-ee-ics-technologies-and-practices-to-displace-decentrilized-thermal-energy-tpddtec-consumption/) or simply by performing a Google search.

Once you’ve located the document, the next step is to read through it carefully and outline a basic workflow. This involves identifying the sequence of steps, the stakeholders involved, and the data that needs to be monitored at each stage of the process. Most registries follow a standardized set of steps, unless the methodology specifies otherwise.

For Gold Standard, I found a [**step-by-step certification process**](https://www.goldstandard.org/gold-standard-for-the-global-goals/gs4gg-certification-process) (see Figure 1\) provided by the registry. This serves as a foundational workflow and offers helpful context before diving deeper into the specifics of the methodology itself.

![][image1]Figure 1: Step by Step Certification process for Gold Standard Registry 

Based on the certification process and an analysis of the methodology document, the TPDDTEC v4.0 workflow primarily involves three key stakeholders: the **Project Developer**, the **Standard Registry**, and a **Validation and Verification Body (VVB)**.

The process begins with **project planning and design**, a step that is submitted only once per project. At this stage, the project developer prepares and submits essential project information, including stakeholder consultations, the **Project Design Document (PDD)**,  and contributions to the **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**. It's also important to check the **applicability criteria** during this phase to ensure that the specific project aligns with the methodology being digitized.

Once all the submitted details are reviewed and approved by the standard registry, the project is officially listed. The next step involves submitting the project documentation—along with **emission reduction calculation estimates**—to the assigned VVB for **validation** (Step 3). Upon approval from the VVB, the project design is considered certified.

Following certification, the project is implemented in the field. The project developer then submits a **monitoring report**, an **annual report**, and actual **emission reduction calculations**. After these submissions are reviewed and approved by the VVB, the data is forwarded to the standard registry for **final approval** and **carbon credit issuance**.

**Formulating Emission Reduction calculations**

While reviewing the methodology document, it’s important to identify and take note of all the formulas provided for **Emission Reduction (ER) calculations**. Pay close attention to the parameters used in each formula, and categorize them as either **monitoring**, **non-monitoring**, or **user-input values** (i.e., values that the project developer will need to provide during submission).

Methodologies typically specify which parameters must be monitored—meaning data that should be **recorded throughout the project’s crediting period**—and which are non-monitored or fixed.

In the case of **TPDDTEC v4.0**, the methodology outlines **three distinct methods/formulas** for calculating emission reductions, depending on the **fuel type** used. During my review, I focused especially on identifying the **monitoring parameters**, how they are derived or calculated, and what data the user will be expected to input.

Once these elements are clear, the next step is to begin **building the schemas** that align with both the workflow and the corresponding ER calculations.

**Generating Schemas for the policy**

Once you're familiar with the methodology and have a clear understanding of the workflow and how the **Emission Reduction (ER)** calculations are structured, the next step is to start developing the **schemas**.

If you're already familiar with the **Guardian platform**, you’ll know that it organizes schemas in a **schema tree** structure. This includes high-level schemas—typically the main schema visible to the user that incorporates multiple **low-level (or sub-)schemas**—and low-level schemas that handle specific parts of a calculation or questionnaire.

It’s generally a good idea to **start with the calculation schemas**, as these are the core of any methodology. Begin by creating the low-level schemas and work your way up to the high-level ones. For instance, in **TPDDTEC**, there are three distinct methods defined for ER calculations as mentioned before. So, I started by creating each of these methods as separate **sub-schemas**.

Schemas are typically created in **Excel**, and you don't need to build them entirely from scratch. Guardian provides a ready-made template, which you can download by clicking the **“Generate Excel Template”** button under the **“Manage Schemas”** section (see Fig. 2). If you want to review schema structures from other methodologies, you can export them by clicking **“Export Schemas to Excel”** under a specific policy in the **“Manage Policy”** section (see Fig. 3).

![][image2]  
Figure 2: Download Excel Template for schemas

![][image3]  
Figure 3: Export policy schemas to excel

**Creating Calculation Schemas**

When working on **Calculation Schemas**, the usual approach is to locate the **Emission Reduction (ER) formula** in the methodology document and work backward from there. If available, finding the **Emission Reduction Tool** for the specific methodology is extremely helpful. These tools typically list all the parameters involved in the ER calculation, indicating:

* Which parameters are **user inputs**

* Which ones have **default standard values**  
* Which need to be **calculated**, along with their formulas

For the TPDDTEC methodology, I used [this](https://globalgoals.goldstandard.org/407-4-tpddtec-er-calculation-tool/) ER tool provided for digitization. If an ER tool is not available, you'll need to manually extract this information from the methodology document.

Since **TPDDTEC defines three distinct methods** for calculating emission reductions, I started with **Method 1**. Because the applicable method depends on project type and other conditions, Method 1 was set up as a **sub-schema**. This allows it to be modular and easily referenced within the **main schema** (such as a **Verifiable Credential** or **Encrypted Verifiable Credential**).

Therefore, in the Excel schema template, you’ll begin by adding the name of your sub-schema at the top and selecting the **Schema Type** as **“Sub-Schema”** (see Figure 4).

![][image4]  
Figure 4: Method 1 sub-schema

Next, you’ll begin **populating the schema** by adding each parameter from the methodology one by one, including all **supporting (often hidden) parameters** that are required to perform the necessary calculations.

For **Method 1**, the equation provided in the methodology is as follows:  
![][image5]

As you can see, the **ERy** formula involves a summation. However, since we're working within a **sub-schema**, we can disregard the summation at this level. Instead, in the **policy workflow**, we’ll provide the **project developer** with the ability to **submit the Monitoring Report**—along with ER calculations—**multiple times**. Then, in the **“Custom Calculation Logic”** block of the policy workflow, we’ll apply the final **Emission Reduction (ER)** calculation across all submitted **Monitoring Report Verifiable Credentials (VCs)** for the year to generate the overall summation as indicated in the methodology formula.

The first step in building this in the Excel schema is to list out the parameters **one by one** and analyze how each is calculated. I began with the parameter **Nb,p,y**, which is defined as:

*“Number of project technology-days included in the project database for baseline b/project p pairs in year y (days).”*

In my approach, I chose to calculate **ERy per day**, and then multiply that value by **365** to get the annual **ERy**. As a result, I fixed **Nb,p,y \= 1** within the sub-schema. Since this is a backend logic detail and should not be exposed to the user, I set the **visibility** of this field to **“Hidden”** (see Figure 5). Hidden fields will not appear during user data entry, but they can still be referenced within the **Custom Logic Block** for backend calculations.

Following this, I moved on to the parameter **Up,y**, which represents the **number of project stoves used**. Unlike Nb,p,y, this value **must be entered by the user**, as it varies from project to project.

   Figure 5: Nb,p,y and Up,y fields in Method 1 sub-schema

**Handling Enum Fields for Parameter Selection**

In some cases, you'll need to use different **field types** for parameters that aren’t straightforward numeric inputs. A good example is the parameter **SFSp,b,y**. As outlined in the methodology, **Method 1** allows for different **primary fuel types**—such as wood, charcoal, etc.—which affects the emission reduction calculation.

To accommodate this in the schema, you’ll need to let users **select the fuel type**, which means the field type should be set to **“enum”**. Enum fields present a predefined list of options to the user.

To implement this:

1. **Create a separate sheet** in your Excel file to hold the enum options.  
2. In your main schema sheet, under the **“Parameter”** column for this field, link to the enum options sheet by providing its name (as shown in **Figures 6 and 7**).  
3. In the enum options sheet, make sure to:

   * Enter the **exact schema name** in the appropriate column (e.g., `"Method 1 for baseline b/project p pair"`).

   * Enter the **exact field name** where the enum will be used (e.g., `"Select the primary fuel type in baseline:"`).

   * Add **`(enum)`** at the end of the sheet name to help Guardian recognize it as an options list.

In the **“Answer”** column of the enum sheet, create a **dropdown list** of the fuel type options available as per the methodology.

Once this is set up, when you import the Excel file into Guardian, it will automatically generate a schema field with the specified dropdown options for the user to choose from (**Figure 8**).

 Figure 6: “Select the primary fuel type in baseline:” field in Method 1 sub-schema

![][image6]  
 Figure 7: Options for “Select the primary fuel type in baseline:” field 

![][image7]

Figure 8: Schema field “Select the primary fuel type in baseline:” in guardian

Once the **Primary Fuel Type** field is configured, we can move on to implementing the **SFSp,b,y** field. This parameter—**Specific Fuel Savings**—is calculated using the following formula:

SFSp,b,y \= **Pb,y** − **Pp,y**

Where:

* **Pb,y** \= Quantity of fuel consumed in the **baseline scenario** during year y  
* **Pp,y** \= Quantity of fuel consumed in the **project scenario** during year y

To implement this in the schema:

1. **Create hidden fields** for both **Pb,y** and **Pp,y** for each potential **fuel type** defined in the methodology.  
2. For each fuel type, create a corresponding **SFSp,b,y** field.  
   * In the **Answer** column of the Excel template, input the formula:  
      `=Pb,y − Pp,y`  
3. Set the **visibility** of the input fields (**Pb,y** and **Pp,y**) to **TRUE or FALSE**, depending on the **fuel type selected** by the user. This ensures that users only see and input values relevant to the selected fuel type.

Finally, create a **main SFSp,b,y field** that selects the correct calculated value based on the chosen primary fuel type. You can do this using a conditional formula like:

\= IF(G11="Wood", G13, IF(G11="Charcoal combustion only", G14, IF(G11="Charcoal combustion and production", G14, IF(G11="Other", G16, G16))))

Where:

* `G11` \= The user's selected primary fuel type  
* `G13`, `G14`, `G16`, etc. \= Corresponding calculated **SFSp,b,y** values for each fuel type

This setup dynamically pulls the correct fuel savings value into the final field based on the user's selection.

**Figure 9** illustrates the complete configuration for calculating **SFSp,b,y**.

![][image8]  
Figure 9: SFSp,b,y fields and its structure

In a similar manner, we proceed to calculate the remaining parameters required in the Method 1 formula, following the same structure—defining fields, applying formulas, and managing visibility where needed.

Once all parameters are in place, we move on to computing the **Net Emission Reductions**. For this, I broke down the calculation into three fields:

* **ER/day** – Daily emission reductions  
* **ER/month** – Monthly emission reductions (optional for reporting convenience)  
* **ER/year** – Annual emission reductions

At this stage, we now have all the required inputs and intermediate values ready, so we can directly apply the **Method 1 formula** in the **“Answer”** column to calculate **ER/day**. Once that’s computed, we simply multiply **ER/day** by **365** to get the final:

**“Net Emission Reductions for total project activity in year y (tCO₂e/year)”**

This gives us the core output for the Method 1 sub-schema.

**Figure 10** shows the complete schema setup for **Method 1** calculations. All the other methods and calculations were done using the template described above.

![][image9]  
Figure 10: Method 1 for baseline b/project p schema excel

**Integrating Calculation Logic into Guardian**

With this, we’ve covered all the calculation schemas for the TPDDTEC methodology. Hopefully, you now have a clearer understanding of how to go about creating an **Emission Reductions schema**.

However, an important point to note: although formulas and calculations are defined within your Excel schema, **these formulas are not automatically imported into Guardian**. When you upload the schema file, all the fields will be present, but the actual **formulas and logic will not carry over**.

To incorporate calculations into your digitized methodology, there are two options. Let’s focus on the first and more commonly used approach:

#### **Option 1: Using a Custom Calculate Block in the Policy Workflow**

In Guardian’s **Policy Workflow Configurator**, you can add a **"Custom Calculate"** block *after* the user's **Verifiable Credential (VC)** document is stored. This block allows you to define and apply all necessary formulas and calculations using code.

* The **input** to this block is the raw VC document containing the user’s submitted data.  
* The **output** is a modified VC document with all calculated fields populated.

At present, **JavaScript** is the only supported language for writing custom logic in this block. However, support for other languages (like Python) is planned in future updates.

Here’s a high-level view of how this works:

* Define your custom logic using JavaScript within the Calculate block.  
* Use the input document’s fields, apply your calculation logic, and assign the result back to the output document (usually the same schema with the calculated fields populated).  
* This enables dynamic computation at runtime based on user-submitted data.

The block structure and a sample code snippet in the Policy Configurator would look like this:

Figure 11: Block structure with input document being passed to calculate\_project\_fields block (top-left), Properties of the calculate\_project\_fields block with the expression and output schema configured (top-right), Code in the expression field of the calculate\_project\_fields (bottom-center)  
**Option 2: Using the “Auto Calculate” field type in the schema**

Another powerful way to incorporate calculations—arguably a more streamlined and low-code option—is by using the **“Auto Calculate”** field type directly in the Excel schema.

In this approach:

* You set the **Field Type** of any parameter you want to be auto-calculated as **"Auto Calculate"**.  
* Then, in the **“Answer”** column for that field, you include the formula (just like you would in a normal Excel formula).  
* When you import the schema into Guardian, the platform will **automatically recognize the formula**, and a **custom calculation block** will be generated for you behind the scenes—no additional manual coding needed in the workflow configurator.

This is especially useful for straightforward calculations that don’t require conditional logic or complex branching. It also saves time by removing the need to write JavaScript in the policy workflow manually.

Guardian’s documentation provides more details about [this feature](https://docs.hedera.com/guardian-dev-1/guardian/standard-registry/import-export-in-excel/import-and-export-excel-file-user-guide#id-3.-calculations-and-math-expressions), along with guidance on importing/exporting schema Excel files.

Both methods—**Custom Calculate Block** and **Auto Calculate Field Type**—are valid and can be used depending on the complexity of your logic and your comfort with coding. More details about the calculation block in the policy workflow engine is provided towards the end of the blog.

**Generating a Schema Tree Structure in Excel**

Now that we've completed the calculation schemas, it's important to note that these are often **not the only schemas** required at a given stage in the policy workflow.

For example, during the **Project Listing** stage, in addition to collecting **projected Emission Reduction estimates**, you’ll typically also want to capture other project-related data—such as:

* **Project details**  
* **Stakeholder consultation information**  
* **Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) contributions**  
* And any other required metadata

To handle this, the standard approach is to **combine all relevant sub-schemas** into a **main schema**, which usually represents a **Verifiable Credential (VC)**. This main schema is what the user will fill out during that stage, allowing them to input all required details in a single step.

For example, a finalized schema tree for the **Project Listing** stage may look something like **Figure 12**, where you can see the **Method 1 schema** included as one of the nested (or “low-level”) sub-schemas we created earlier.

In the next section, we’ll walk through how to **combine multiple sub-schemas** into a single **Verifiable Credential schema**, and configure it so it’s ready to be used in Guardian’s workflow.

![][image10]  
Figure 12: Schema tree of Project Description Schema as seen in Guardian platform

As discussed earlier, the **Project Listing** stage will consist of three main components:

1. **Key Project Information / Project Details**  
2. **Stakeholder Consultation Information**  
3. **Emission Reductions Estimates**

All of this will be submitted by the **Project Developer** and later validated by the **VVB (Validation and Verification Body)**.

We’ve already created the calculation schemas for **Method 1**, **Method 2**, and **Method 3**. Now, to allow the user to select which calculation method applies to their project, we’ll create a new sub-schema named **Emission Reductions**. This sub-schema will act as a container for the three method-specific sub-schemas.

To implement this:

* Add an **Enum field** (just like we did previously) that allows the user to choose between Method 1, Method 2, and Method 3\.  
* Then, create a row for each of the three methods within the **Emission Reductions** sub-schema.  
* Under each method row, add the corresponding fields (or sub-schemas) related to that specific method.  
* Use Excel’s **grouping feature** to visually and logically group the fields under each method row (as shown in Figure 13).

This setup allows Guardian to dynamically show or hide specific method fields based on the user’s selection from the Enum dropdown, creating a more guided and user-friendly input experience.

![][image11]  
Figure 13: Emission Reductions sub-schema with all methods sub-schemas incorporated and grouped. Method 3 (Non Fossil Fuel) grouped fields are visible here

Once the **Emission Reductions** sub-schema is created, the next step is to incorporate it—along with all other relevant sub-schemas—into a **main Verifiable Credential schema**, which we’ll call **Project Description**.

To do this:

* Copy the entire content of the **Emission Reductions** sub-schema into the **Project Description VC** schema. Since it's also a sub-schema, it will be nested accordingly (as shown in Figure 14).  
* Repeat this process for **all other sub-schemas**—including Key Project Information and Stakeholder Consultation—so that everything required for the **Project Listing** step is included in one unified VC.

This ensures that when the Excel file is imported into Guardian, the schema tree remains structurally intact and the nesting is preserved correctly. You’ll be able to visualize and manage this tree structure directly within Guardian.

Finally, you’ll configure this **Project Description VC schema** for use in the **Project Listing** stage of your digitized policy workflow.

![][image12]  
Figure 14: Project Description Verifiable Credential schema with all the sub-schemas and their fields incorporated.

In a similar manner, all other necessary schemas and sub-schemas are created, completing the schema development phase of the methodology digitization process. With the full schema tree in place, the next step is to **import these schemas into Guardian** and begin building the **policy workflow** using the **Policy Configurator** tool available on the platform.

**Policy creation using guardian policy configurator**

You can either **create a policy from scratch** or **import an existing policy** from Guardian's Methodology Library and customize it according to your specific workflow. For the purpose of this blog, we’ll be creating a policy from scratch, while occasionally referring to other digitized methodology policies for guidance.

Once you create a new, empty policy, begin by providing **basic metadata** such as the policy name, description, version, and other relevant details (see Figure 15). After this, import the **final Excel schema document** you prepared earlier. Upon import, Guardian will flag any **errors or warnings** within the schema—review and resolve these to ensure a clean import.

With all schemas successfully imported into the policy, you're now ready to begin **constructing the policy workflow**.

If you are not familiar with the guardian policy workflow engine, in brief it manages and monitors the state of required policy actions and the required information flow in a policy grouped into a policy workflow, and determines which are the next policy actions based on the state of a policy workflow. The policy actions may be anything from saving an application form in a document management system to sending a reminder email to users or escalating overdue items to management. Policy Workflow Blocks enable different business logic to exist within the Guardian’s PWE. We will use the different types of policy workflow blocks available and configure our policy workflow from start to end for each stakeholder. Information of all different blocks are provided in the [guardian documentation](https://docs.hedera.com/guardian-dev-1/guardian/standard-registry/policies/policy-creation/introduction). 

Let’s now dive into the **policy building process**. I’ll walk you through how I created the TPDDTEC v4.0 policy, while also generalizing the steps so that you can apply the same logic when digitizing any methodology using Guardian’s Policy Workflow Configurator.

![][image13]

Figure 15: Policy workflow configurator

**Configuring User Roles in the policy**

First step of almost any policy would be to allow the users to be able to choose a role that they are gonna be playing in the policy workflow. We can add all the possible stakeholders in the “Roles” section of the policy so they will be available for us to choose in the policy blocks (Figure 16).

![][image14]   
Figure 16: Roles section of the policy

Once all the roles are added, we will add a “policyRolesBlock” to the configurator and different “interfaceContainerBlock” blocks for each stakeholder. For my policy, since the role of “NGO Supporters” and “Technical Advisory Committee” users is to review the documents and have the ability to comment, I merged it in one “interfaceContainerBlock” block. But since “Project Proponent”, “VVB” and “Gold Standard” registry have different permissions and different interfaces, a separate “interfaceContainerBlock” is created for each stakeholder. In the “Choose Roles” block all 4 users except for the “Gold Standard” are selected since we don't want any user to assume a registry role as only the owner of the policy or specified user can have a registry role (Figure 17). “Default Active” option will also be checked as this is the first step the user should complete in the policy workflow.

![][image15]  
Figure 17: “policyRolesBlock” configuration

**Project Proponent Workflow**

Since Project Proponent is the one that will drive this project, we will configure the workflow for this stakeholder first and then the other stakeholders are connected to the Project Proponent as the data submitted by the project proponent will be sent to stakeholders like VVB and registry for review. 

We have already created an empty  “interfaceContainerBlock” block for Project proponent already named “Project\_Proponent\_header” and according to the finalised workflow we will add three more “interfaceContainerBlock” blocks under Project Proponent header named “Projects\_pp”, “Monitoring\_Reports\_pp” and token to denote the project listing phase, monitoring phase and the token issuance phase of the workflow (Figure 18). One thing to remember is roles that are permitted to access these blocks. For example currently we are working under the “Project\_Proponent\_header” interface so we will add Project\_Proponent role in the “Permissions” property so that any block under it will propagate the same permissions and other users are not allowed to access/alter these blocks

![][image16]  
Figure 18: Project Proponent’s Project, Monitoring Report and Token interface tabs

Once the Project Proponent signs up, we would want them to be able to see an “Add Project” button by default to start the project listing process. For this a “requestVcDocumentBlock” can be used and you can provide details to it such as the Button Title, the type you want to show the users (you can select between a dialog and page). Further you would select an appropriate schema to show when the button is clicked. Since in excel “Project Description” Schema is created, we will select it for this button. Now that we have the UI component set, we would need to save the data filled in the schema by the user and for that we will use the server component "sendToGuardianBlock" in order to save the VC document. The data can be sent to hedera blockchain as a message which will live as an immutable entry of the submission and can also be saved in the guardian database. Here for transparency we will send the VC to Hedera blockchain and save it in the database as well. So we add two "sendToGuardianBlock" blocks, one where we add the dataSource to be “Hedera” and since we want every project to have their own hedera topic for better structure we will create a topic named “Project” and add that topic in the block’s properties. Second "sendToGuardianBlock" block’s dataSource would be a database (Figure 19). 

Figure 19: save\_project\_hedera and save\_project "sendToGuardianBlock" and their properties

Once the project description is submitted, as per the workflow it needs to reach the registry user for review and listing of the project and once listed it needs to be sent to VVB for validation process hence we need to track the progress of the document and monitor its state changes. For the same we will attach an attribute of “status” with the project description VC so we are able to track its state. For this you can add “Option” in the properties of the "sendToGuardianBlock" block (Figure 20). Here I will add a status option with the value “Waiting to be listed”. Once this is completed, we now have the VC document but in order to show it and its status in the UI we will add an “interfaceDocumentsSourceBlock” and I have named the block “project\_grid\_pp\_2” which will contain the document source with the appropriate schema selected and filter added according to the “status” attribute that we defined earlier (Figure 21). We will add a “RefreshEvent” in the “save\_project” block to send to the “interfaceDocumentsSourceBlock” so that when the VC document is saved, a refresh is triggered on the UI and the document is fetched.

![][image17]  
Figure 20: Add option attribute in order to track state of the document.

![][image18]  
Figure 21: “interfaceDocumentsSourceBlock” UI component configuration  
Next would be to incorporate the calculations after the submission of the data from the user. As mentioned above there are 2 ways of doing calculations, here we are going to make use of the “customLogicBlock” to define the formulas (Figure 22). This block will be added right after the “save\_project” block so that it can receive the document submitted by the user and in this block’s properties you can choose an output schema. Since we have the fields that need to be calculated in the same schema, I will choose “Project Description” Schema here. In the expression property is where you will add the code/logic for the calculation.  

![][image19]  
Figure 22: customLogicBlock and its properties

In the expression property, you can access the input VC document directly from the documents array as shown in figure 23 and get its “credentialSubject” which are the fields that you can access and use to calculate fields like Net Emission Reductions etc. For TPDDTEC policy, since I have 3 methods, I decided to structure each method in its own function and call the function depending on the method that is selected by the project proponent. In each method call, I set the appropriate field with the calculated value based on the formula that we added in the excel. At the end you would call the main function to trigger the logic (calc() in my case). Currently the custom logic block only supports javascript programming language but support for other languages such as R is also in guardian’s development pipeline. After the calculation block, we want the document to be saved hence we will add the "sendToGuardianBlock" blocks as mentioned above to save the document.  

Figure 23: Logic added in the expression property of “customLogicBlock”

Now our work for project description submission is complete and similarly we will create the workflow for submission of monitoring reports as well (Figure 24). 

![][image20]  
Figure 24: Finalised project and report flow for project proponent

Next part would be to add the registry and VVB part so that they could review the documents and perform certain operations to change the document’s status but we will look at that in the respective registry and VVB workflow section. Let's assume we have submitted the project description document and sent it to the registry for review and listing. Once the registry approves the project and lists it, we want to send it to a Validation and Verification Body (VVB) entity for validation of our project design. There can be a number of different VVB users that are associated with the policy and we don't want all the VVB users to see the project information and perform certain actions hence we will need to add an assign functionality to assign the VC documents to a certain VVB. For this we will use “interfaceActionBlock” and add a VVB schema “documentSourceAddOnBlock” to it and make it a dropdown menu with one of the fields from the VVB schema (we will take VVB name field in this case) to be shown in the dropdown. So in the “interfaceActionBlock” we will add a Button name and give dropdown options for the user to choose a VVB (Figure 25\)

![][image21]  
Figure 25:  assign\_vvb interfaceActionBlock’s configuration

One thing to notice is since all the details and documents of the project are sent to the blockchain, it is immutable and hence impossible to edit the document once it is on the Hedera blockchain. So if by mistake there is wrong data submitted there is no way to rollback. In this case we added a “Revoke” functionality which basically changes the status of the project document to “Revoked” and all the other functionality are configured according to that. Similarly the “Revoke” functionality was added for VVB documents. Monitoring reports etc. 

Once the project is validated by the VVB, the next step would be to submit the monitoring report hence, the way I have configured the workflow is to have a separate UI tab to show the monitoring reports. Once the project is validated, in the document entry we will see the “Add Report” button which we added in the new\_report “interfaceStepBlock” as seen in Figure 23\. But in order for it to be shown on the document entry we would also add it in the project\_grid\_pp\_2 “interfaceDocumentsSourceBlock” (Figure 26). Similarly we added a button to view all the reports of the project in the Monitoring Reports tab so as seen in Figure 25 we will add a field in the project\_grid\_pp\_2 “interfaceDocumentsSourceBlock” properties to show the button. Then we will configure the “Monitoring\_Reports\_pp” tab to add the report\_grid\_pp interfaceDocumentsSourceBlock with all the document sources with respective status. Here we will also add a filter block to filter the reports by the project id (Figure 27).  

![][image22]  
Figure 26: UI for the Project description VC after its listed by registry and validated by VVB

![][image23]  
Figure 27: Monitoring\_reports\_pp interface configuration

Final step of the Project Proponent workflow is to view the token history and the carbon credit tokens that are issued once the project is validated and verified and the tokens are minted by the registry. This would just be a simple step where we add the  “interfaceDocumentsSourceBlock” and add a document source with a field type as “mint” (Figure 28\)

Figure 28: tokens "interfaceContainerBlock" configuration (on the left) and tokens\_grid\_tokens "documentsSourceAddon" block properties (on the right)

**Standard Registry (Gold Standard) Workflow**

Standard Registry will play several key roles in the project development and progression starting from VVB approval, project listing and carbon credits issuance. Hence, similar to what we did in Project Proponent workflow, we will create separate “interfaceContainerBlock” tabs for VVB approval, project pipeline and monitoring reports. Additionally we will also create a tab for “Verifiable Presentation” and “Trust Chain” which I will explain in the later section.

For VVB approval, similar to what we did for Project Proponent, we will add an “interfaceDocumentsSourceBlock” named “vvb\_grid\_gs” which will contain the document source with the appropriate schema (VVB schema) selected and filter added according to the “status” attribute that we defined earlier and “type” with the value “vvb” (Figure 29). We will also add “buttonBlock” which can be used to add two buttons approve and reject. On approval we will change the state of the VVB document and on rejection we will open a dialog asking for the reason for rejection (Figure 30). Along with this, if the VVB is approved by mistake then we would also add the “Revoke” functionality in the similar way that we did in the Project Proponent workflow (Figure 31).

![][image24]  
Figure 29: VVB approval workflow configuration for standard registry

**![][image25]**  
Figure 30: Approve and Reject button configuration for the VVB application

Next, we will configure the Project Listing workflow for standard registry. For this again we will have the “interfaceDocumentsSourceBlock” named “project\_grid\_gs” which will contain the document source with the appropriate schema (Project Description schema) selected and filter added according to the “status” attribute. Along with that we will add a “List” button which on clicking will move the status of the Project Description VC from “Waiting to be Listed” to “Waiting for Validation” which will be the step done by the VVB user. Figure 31 shows the UI that will be visible to the standard registry user. If you remember, we added “Revoke” functionality for the project and project proponents can revoke the project even after it is listed hence we have to also propagate the revoke to remove the project document from the Standard Registry user as it is revoked by the project proponent. We can achieve that by not adding a document source in the project\_grid\_gs block which has a status attribute of “Revoked” and then it would simply not show in the UI.

![][image26]  
Figure 31: Project Listing UI for Standard Registry user

We also need to configure the registry workflow for Monitoring Reports (MR) and have a flow where the registry will approve or reject the MRs and if approved then they will also have an option to mint the amount of tokens that are configured (For this policy it will be taken from the value of the Net Emission Reductions field in the MR). The Monitoring Reports workflow will be very similar again, we will have a “interfaceDocumentsSourceBlock” and all the appropriate document sources inside it. Along with it we will have Approve or Reject buttons which will change the state of the VC accordingly and on click of “Reject” button, we will open a text dialog for the standard registry to add a remark on why the Monitoring Report is rejected (Figure 32). If approved, the next and final step of the workflow is credit issuance. Hence we would need to add minting functionality. We can create a token for our policy which will be on the hedera blockchain and act as a carbon credit. So the final Net Emission number that is calculated in the Monitoring Report can be used to mint the same amount of tokens to the project developer.

![][image27]  
Figure 32: Monitoring Report workflow configuration for standard registry user

Let's look at how to configure the mint functionality. First we will add a buttonBlock (here I have named it mint\_token\_gs). To this button we will add a button in properties and on click we will set the status to “Minting”. Next we will add “sendToGuardianBlock” to save the status of “Minting” in the blockchain/database. After that we will add a “mintDocumentBlock” which will start the token minting process. In its properties you can select the token you want to mint (the dropdown will show all the tokens present within the guardian). Under “Rule” property, you can add the field from where you want to get the amount of tokens that need to be issued and “Account Type” properties can be set to default which will be the standard registry Hedera account, “Custom Account field” where you can again add the field from schema if there is the account number present to which you want to mint the tokens or “Custom Account Value” where you can directly add the account id to which the token needs to be minted. Once the minting is completed we will again add a  “sendToGuardianBlock” to save the mint status as minted (Figure 33).

![][image28]  
Figure 33: Mint functionality configuration for the standard registry user

Once we have minted the token, the guardian issues a verifiable presentation (VP) document. A Verifiable Presentation is a combination of all the Verifiable Credentials, bundled together into a format. So this can help us trace all the documents that were submitted to the guardian for a particular project and their timeline. For the same purpose we will create a “Trust Chain” UI in the guardian. The first step would be to add ​​”reportBlock” inside which we will add all the “reportItemBlock” to keep a record of all the VC. In Guardian policy ​​”reportBlock” finds VP documents based on their id or hash and displays document information, related policy and token information. To retrieve the linked information “reportItemBlock” must be used. If you remember we added status and entity attributes at each VC step throughout the policy and that will help us with configuring the Trust Chain (Figure 34).

As we can see in Figure 34, first we will add the MintTokenItem and to find this document which describes the token mint operation we will use one of the Common Variables i.e. actionId. Similarly to all the other VC documents we can filter out and find it through either the Entity attribute or the type (root \-\> type). In order for blockchain to also keep a trace of all the document submission in correct order as well as to be able to trace the project documents from start to end using the hedera messages, guardian has “relationships” which can be retrieved through the hedera messages using the messageId . For example, in the MintTokenItem, we set the variable named “mint\_token\_relationships” and its value as root-\> relationships. In the “ReportMonitoringReportApproved” we will add a filter to look for “mint\_token\_relationships” in the messageId. This is another way to retrieve the VC document through relationships. This should give us a full trust chain of the project with all the VC documents, credit issuances, VP document, policy details etc. (Figure 35\)

![][image29]  
Figure 34: Trust Chain configuration

![][image30]  
Figure 35: Trust Chain User Interface

Hopefully, after going through this detailed walkthrough, you now have a solid understanding of how to configure a policy workflow and are familiar with the core Policy Workflow Blocks available in Guardian. Keep in mind that what we’ve covered here is just the beginning—Guardian offers a wide range of powerful workflow blocks and features that enable even more advanced configurations and automation.

The workflows for other stakeholders such as **Validation and Verification Bodies (VVBs)**, **NGOs**, and **supporting entities** are set up in a similar fashion as the one we've detailed here for **Project Developers** and the **Standard Registry**. With this foundational knowledge, you’re well-equipped to start digitizing methodologies and designing robust, transparent, and traceable carbon policies using Guardian.
